Opinion
    
             

    Premium  

    
    
                        
            Tanya Sweeney
                    Opinion        After entering a Facebook raffle to win a house and car, I've resolved never to gamble €100 like that again
    

            
            
    Whenever my dad and I are in the pub, talk turns occasionally to what we might do with a EuroMillions win. He would travel the world forever; I would spend the lot on houses with sea views and spend winter in Australia. Last week, Dad said something that snagged on my brain. “I’ll never do the lottery,” he said. “You can keep your good luck. I don’t need it. Just don’t visit me with bad luck.” What he meant was that, as long as he wasn’t beset by tragedy or misfortune, he would consider himself doing just fine in life. There’s definitely something in this.

Life
    
             

    Premium  

    
    
                        
    exclusive
        Yewande Biala book extract: ‘I felt like I had to make people believe I was living the dream’
    

            
            
    Social media does a great job of creating an optical illusion of reality, or maybe we create this ourselves, by posting a reality we want people to see. I’ve always been able to differentiate between social media and real life. Although, on some occasions, I find myself deep on my ‘explore’ page wondering why I don’t have a millionaire boyfriend who wants to shower me with gifts and fly me away for date nights in Turks and Caicos. But, somehow, I seem to always shake myself back into reality and remind myself that, even though a picture might tell a thousand words, those words are none of my business.
